unit.name: Broader discussion: transfer, deployment, language interfaces, and neuroscience
unit.description: Uses audience questions to generalize the lecture’s themes across modalities, safety-critical deployment, human-language interfaces, and links to neuroscience.
unit.summary: In discussion, Kim distinguishes between domain-general interpretability theory and application-specific deployment work: proofs about tools like SHAP and IG may transfer across modalities, but practical systems still require customized validation in context [ts=3739s] [ts=3770s]. She argues that interpretability tools are especially valuable for surfacing unexpected failure modes before deployment, since real risk often comes from surprises rather than known test cases [ts=3892s] [ts=3938s]. On human-language interfaces, she says mapping only human concepts into machine space can help but will inevitably miss machine-native concepts like Move 37, so progress likely requires both translating existing human language and learning new vocabulary from machines [ts=3979s] [ts=4014s] [ts=4073s]. Finally, she connects interpretability to neuroscience while warning that brain-inspired neuron-wise thinking may reflect tooling limits rather than the right abstraction for neural networks, where whole embeddings and transformations can be probed directly [ts=4169s] [ts=4230s] [ts=4243s].

key_points.text: Some interpretability theory transfers across modalities, but application-specific validation remains necessary.
A major deployment goal is discovering surprises before real users are harmed.
Mapping human concepts into machine space is helpful but incomplete because machines may have important concepts humans lack words for.
Neuroscience may bias interpretability toward single-neuron explanations because biological probing tools are limited.
In artificial networks, whole embeddings and transformations can be intervened on directly, not just individual neurons.

unit_summary_for_generation: In discussion, Kim distinguishes between domain-general interpretability theory and application-specific deployment work: proofs about tools like SHAP and IG may transfer across modalities, but practical systems still require customized validation in context [ts=3739s] [ts=3770s]. She argues that interpretability tools are especially valuable for surfacing unexpected failure modes before deployment, since real risk often comes from surprises rather than known test cases [ts=3892s] [ts=3938s]. On human-language interfaces, she says mapping only human concepts into machine space can help but will inevitably miss machine-native concepts like Move 37, so progress likely requires both translating existing human language and learning new vocabulary from machines [ts=3979s] [ts=4014s] [ts=4073s]. Finally, she connects interpretability to neuroscience while warning that brain-inspired neuron-wise thinking may reflect tooling limits rather than the right abstraction for neural networks, where whole embeddings and transformations can be probed directly [ts=4169s] [ts=4230s] [ts=4243s].

kp_catalog.name: Cross-domain transfer and human-language interfaces
kp_catalog.description: Interpretability methods mix domain-general theory with domain-specific deployment, and human-language interfaces help but cannot fully capture machine-native concepts.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because cross-domain transfer and human-language interfaces clarifies interpretability methods mix domain-general theory with domain-specific deployment, and human-language interfaces help but cannot fully capture machine-native concepts, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Limits of attribution methods for behavioral inference
kp_catalog.description: Saliency, IG, and SHAP may look plausible but often do not justify conclusions about what a model actually uses or how it will behave.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because limits of attribution methods for behavioral inference formalizes how saliency, IG, and SHAP may look plausible but often do not justify conclusions about what a model actually uses or how it will behave, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Shared representational overlap
kp_catalog.description: Humans and machines need partially overlapping representations or vocabulary to exchange meaning and collaborate effectively.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because shared representational overlap formalizes how humans and machines need partially overlapping representations or vocabulary to exchange meaning and collaborate effectively, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
